content.text:
<user_query>
Describe for me where the cursor rules are that you use to know how to set node version and run tasks and things like that.
</user_query>

Here is where those instructions live and how they show up for me.

## Workspace Cursor rules

**Toolchain (Node, pnpm, how to run checks)**  
- Path: [`.cursor/rules/toolchain.mdc`](file:///Users/duhl/git/ui/.cursor/rules/toolchain.mdc)  
- It tells me to use **mise** (not nvm), to align with **`mise.toml`** at the repo root, and to prefer **`pnpm run validate`** for a full pre-merge check (lint ‚Üí build ‚Üí typecheck ‚Üí test ‚Üí e2e).

**Other rules**  
- Anything under **`.cursor/rules/`** can be picked up as Cursor rules (often `.mdc` files). Your repo has at least `toolchain.mdc` there.

## Repo-level agent instructions

**`AGENTS.md`** at the repo root ([`AGENTS.md`](file:///Users/duhl/git/ui/AGENTS.md)) is also applied as workspace guidance‚Äîfor example React form typing (`SubmitEvent` vs `FormEvent`) and string literals.

## Canonical versions on disk

The toolchain rule points to **`mise.toml`** (and **`package.json`** `engines.node` if present) as the source of truth for **which** Node/pnpm versions to use; the **`.mdc` file** is what tells me **to** use mise and those files, not nvm.

So: **behavior for ‚Äúhow to set Node and run tasks‚Äù is primarily in `.cursor/rules/toolchain.mdc`**, with versions defined in **`mise.toml`** (and related package metadata).

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
Okay, let's make sure that anything in dot cursor is added to the.gitignore, so we're not checking that in.
</user_query>

Checking the root `.gitignore` and adding `.cursor/` if missing.

[REDACTED]

Adding `.cursor/` to `.gitignore` under a short Cursor section.

[REDACTED]

input.new_string:
# AI
# *.claude
*.claude

# Cursor IDE (local rules, MCP cache, etc.)
.cursor/

input.description: List tracked files under .cursor if any

content.text:
Here‚Äôs what changed.

**Update:** `.cursor/` is listed in the root [`.gitignore`](file:///Users/duhl/git/ui/.gitignore) under a short ‚ÄúCursor IDE‚Äù comment so the whole directory is ignored (rules, MCP metadata, etc.).

**Note:** Nothing under `.cursor` is tracked in git right now, so you don‚Äôt need `git rm --cached`. If you ever commit `.cursor` first and *then* add this rule, you‚Äôd remove it from the index with:

`git rm -r --cached .cursor`

**Tradeoff:** Team-shared Cursor rules usually live in the repo; ignoring `.cursor` means each developer keeps rules locally or you document conventions elsewhere (e.g. `AGENTS.md`, `mise.toml`). If you wanted shared rules in git, you‚Äôd use a path like `docs/cursor-rules/` and copy or symlink‚Äînot required unless you say otherwise.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
Okay, now as cursor, how would you run a type check real quickly on the org next app? Let's go ahead and do that.
</user_query>

Checking how `org-next` defines its typecheck script.

[REDACTED]

Running the package's `typecheck` script (`tsc --noEmit`) via mise from the repo root:

[REDACTED]

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && mise exec -- pnpm --filter org-next typecheck
input.description: Run TypeScript check for org-next app

content.text:
Checking `mise.toml` and retrying with full permissions so mise can use the correct Node version.

[REDACTED]

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && mise exec -- node -v && mise exec -- pnpm --filter org-next typecheck
input.description: Verify Node via mise and run org-next typecheck

content.text:
Installing the toolchain ‚Äî mise is configured for Node 25.3.0 but the shell is still using Node 16.

[REDACTED]

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && mise install && mise exec -- node -v && mise exec -- pnpm --filter org-next typecheck
input.description: Install mise toolchain, then run org-next typecheck

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && mise which node 2>/dev/null; mise ls; type node; command -v node; echo "---"; PATH="$(mise env -s bash 2>/dev/null | grep -E '^export PATH=' | head -1)" bash -c 'echo $PATH' 2>/dev/null; eval "$(mise activate bash)" 2>/dev/null; node -v
input.description: Diagnose mise node resolution vs PATH
